section.title: Grace to Paul and Timothy's Charge
section.sectionRationale: Having named the gospel entrusted to him, Paul turns it on himself: he, the former blasphemer and persecutor, is living proof of it. Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom Paul is the worst. This overflow of grace fuels a doxology and then a fresh charge to Timothy to fight the good fight, holding faith and a good conscience.
section.thesis: The gospel Paul guards is the very gospel that saved him, the worst of sinners; the mercy shown to him displays Christ's perfect patience as a pattern for all who believe, and it arms Timothy to fight the good fight without shipwrecking his faith.
section.frameNote: Three movements: mercy poured out on Paul the former blasphemer; the trustworthy saying that Christ came to save sinners, sealed with a doxology; and the charge to Timothy to wage the good warfare.

units.label: Mercy for the worst of sinners

verses.text: I thank Christ Jesus our Lord, who has strengthened me, that He considered me faithful and appointed me to service.
I was formerly a {{blasphemos|blasphemer}}, a persecutor, and a violent man; yet because I had acted in ignorance and unbelief, I was shown {{eleos|mercy}}.
And the {{charis|grace}} of our Lord overflowed to me, along with the faith and love that are in Christ Jesus.
This is a {{pistos|trustworthy saying}}, worthy of full acceptance: Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom I am the worst.

But for this very reason I was shown mercy, so that in me, the worst of sinners, Christ Jesus might display His perfect {{makrothymia|patience}} as an example to those who would believe in Him for eternal life.
Now to the King eternal, immortal, and invisible, the only God, be honor and glory forever and ever. Amen.
Timothy, my child, I entrust you with this command in keeping with the previous prophecies about you, so that by them you may {{strateuomai|fight the good fight}},
holding on to faith and a good conscience, which some have rejected and thereby {{nauageo|shipwrecked}} their faith.
Among them are Hymenaeus and Alexander, whom I have handed over to Satan to be taught not to blaspheme.

blasphemos.gloss: A blasphemer, one who reviles and slanders, especially against God. Paul owns this label of his old life as a persecutor of Christ's people. That the chief blasphemer became a herald of the gospel is the sharpest proof of the mercy he describes.

eleos.gloss: Mercy, compassion shown to the undeserving and miserable. Paul says he was shown mercy twice over, in verses 13 and 16. Where grace gives what is not earned, mercy withholds the judgment that is due; both flooded over the former persecutor.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's free and undeserved favor. Paul says the grace of our Lord overflowed, a word picturing a flood beyond measure, carrying faith and love with it. Grace is not merely enough for Paul's sin; it superabounds over it.

pistos.gloss: A trustworthy or faithful saying, a formula marking a settled, reliable summary of the gospel, found five times in the Pastoral Epistles. Here it introduces the heart of the good news: Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners.

makrothymia.gloss: Patience, long-suffering, a slowness to anger that bears long with offenders. Christ displayed His perfect patience in saving even the worst of sinners, making Paul a living pattern, an example, of hope for everyone who will believe.

strateuomai.gloss: To serve as a soldier, to wage war. Paul casts the ministry as warfare: Timothy is to fight the good fight, or wage the good warfare, backed by the prophecies once spoken over him. The Christian life and ministry are a real battle, not a leisure.

nauageo.gloss: To suffer shipwreck, to be dashed and broken as a ship on the rocks. Some who cast off faith and a good conscience have made shipwreck of their faith. The vivid image warns that abandoning conscience wrecks doctrine and life together.

basic.context: Paul thanks Christ for appointing him to ministry, even though he had been a blasphemer, persecutor, and violent man. He was shown mercy and grace overflowed to him. Then comes the great summary of the gospel: Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom Paul is the worst. This bursts into praise, then Paul charges Timothy to fight the good fight and keep faith and a good conscience.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul turns his own story into a pattern. The point is not that Paul is uniquely bad but that if Christ saved the worst, no one is beyond hope. The charge to Timothy is prescriptive: hold faith and a good conscience, or risk shipwreck, as some had done.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul the former blasphemos, blasphemer, was shown eleos, mercy, and charis, grace, that overflowed. The pistos ho logos, the trustworthy saying, sums up the gospel, and Christ's makrothymia, perfect patience, makes Paul a pattern. Timothy must strateuomai, wage the good warfare, lest, like some, he nauageo, shipwreck his faith.
basic.typology: Paul the chief of sinners saved by mercy is a living example, a pattern, of every believer's rescue. The King eternal, immortal, and invisible is glimpsed here and made known fully in Christ, the image of the invisible God.
basic.christ: Verse 15 is the golden center: Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners. He came, He saves, and the worst are not excluded. His perfect patience toward Paul is offered as proof and pattern for all who will believe in Him for eternal life.
basic.god: God is the King eternal, immortal, and invisible, the only God, worthy of honor and glory forever. He is rich in mercy and grace, taking a violent persecutor and making him a herald. His patience is not weakness but saving power aimed at faith.
basic.crossReferences: First Corinthians 15 recalls Paul as one untimely born, the least of the apostles. Romans 5 says where sin increased, grace increased all the more. Second Timothy 4 pictures the ministry as a fight finished and a race run.
basic.oneStory: The gospel that runs through the whole Bible reaches its point here: Christ came to save sinners. Paul's rescue is a signpost of God's mercy to the undeserving, and the charge to fight the good fight hands that gospel on to the next generation, part of God's long work of saving and keeping a people through His Son.

context.literary: Paul moves from the gospel entrusted to him, verse 11, to the gospel that saved him, weaving testimony, creed, and doxology. The trustworthy saying in verse 15 is the first of five such formulas in the Pastorals. The doxology in verse 17 caps the personal section before the renewed charge in verse 18 returns to Timothy and the false teachers.
context.historical: Paul recalls his past as a persecutor of the church, known from Acts 8 and 9. Hymenaeus and Alexander are named as real men who abandoned the faith; handing over to Satan reflects the church's serious discipline, aimed not at destruction but at repentance.
context.canonical: This passage joins Paul's other autobiographical confessions and stands as a template of conversion by sheer grace. The trustworthy saying became a treasured summary of the gospel across the church's history.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground the gospel Paul guards in his own experience of mercy, to hold himself out as a pattern of hope for all sinners, and to charge Timothy to wage the good warfare while holding faith and a good conscience.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul's testimony describes his unique conversion, but he explicitly makes it prescriptive as an example, verse 16: what happened to the worst is a pattern of hope for all. The charge to Timothy, and the warning against shipwreck, are prescriptive for every believer and minister.

debates.question: In what sense is Paul the worst of sinners?

views.label: Genuine present self-assessment
views.case: Paul uses the present tense, I am the worst, in ongoing humility before the God whose grace he never stops marveling at, not as false modesty.

views.label: Chiefly his past as persecutor
views.case: The context, verse 13, roots the claim in his history of blaspheming and persecuting the church, making him the standout trophy of grace.

debates.bearing: Both keep the same point: the mercy that reached the worst leaves no sinner beyond hope, and it keeps the saved humble.

debates.question: What does handing Hymenaeus and Alexander over to Satan mean?

views.label: Formal excommunication
views.case: Putting them outside the protection of the church, into Satan's domain, the world, as in 1 Corinthians 5, so that discipline might bring them to their senses.

views.label: A remedial, not final, judgment
views.case: The stated aim is that they be taught not to blaspheme; the goal is correction and repentance, not damnation.

debates.bearing: Either way church discipline here is serious yet restorative, meant to recover the erring, not merely to punish them.

study.originalLanguages: Paul stacks his old identity: blasphemos, blasphemer, with persecutor and violent man. To such a man came eleos, mercy, the withholding of deserved judgment, and charis, grace, God's free favor, which overflowed, a verb picturing a flood that superabounds. The gospel is announced as a pistos ho logos, a trustworthy saying, the fixed and reliable word. In saving the worst, Christ shows His makrothymia, long-suffering patience, making Paul a pattern for future believers. Timothy is then called to strateuomai, to soldier and wage the good warfare, holding faith and a good conscience, unlike those who nauageo, shipwreck, their faith on the rocks of a rejected conscience.

typology.type: Paul as the pattern trophy of grace
typology.fulfillment: An example, verse 16, of every sinner saved by Christ's perfect patience, showing no one is beyond reach.

typology.type: The King eternal, immortal, and invisible
typology.fulfillment: The invisible God made known in Christ, the image of the invisible God, Colossians 1:15, who came into the world to save.

study.christ: The heart of the passage, and of the letter, is verse 15: Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners. He is the pre-existent Lord who entered the world on a rescue mission, and His saving reach extends even to the worst. His perfect patience toward Paul is set forth as both proof that the gospel is true and a pattern that invites every sinner to believe in Him for eternal life. The doxology to the King eternal flows straight out of wonder at this Savior.
study.god: God is praised as the King eternal, immortal, and invisible, the only God, to whom belong honor and glory forever. He is rich in mercy and lavish in grace, delighting to save the undeserving and to turn a persecutor into a preacher. His patience is saving patience, and His discipline, even handing men over to Satan, aims at their recovery. He is the God who keeps and strengthens those He calls to service.

crossReferences.note: I am the least of the apostles, yet by the grace of God I am what I am; grace was not in vain.
Where sin increased, grace increased all the more, the pattern of Paul's own rescue.
The conversion of Saul the persecutor, the history behind this testimony.
I have fought the good fight, the same warfare image Timothy is charged to take up.

study.oneStory: The long story of Scripture aims at this: Christ came into the world to save sinners. Paul's rescue is a bright signpost of God's mercy to the undeserving, and his charge to Timothy passes the gospel and its warfare to the next generation. It is one chapter in God's unbroken work of saving a people by grace and keeping them through faith and a good conscience until the King eternal is seen face to face.

quiz.q: What is the trustworthy saying at the center of this passage?

options.text: Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners.
options.note: Verse 15 states the gospel in its clearest form.

options.text: God helps those who help themselves.
options.note: This popular slogan is not in the Bible and runs against grace to the undeserving.

options.text: Christ came to reward the righteous for their good lives.
options.note: He came to save sinners, not to reward the self-righteous.

options.text: Only respectable people can be saved.
options.note: Paul, the worst of sinners, is the proof that this is false.

quiz.q: Why was Paul shown mercy, according to verse 16?

options.text: So that Christ might display His perfect patience in him as an example to future believers.
options.note: Paul is made a pattern of hope for all who would believe.

options.text: Because he had earned it by his zeal for the law.
options.note: His zeal made him a persecutor; mercy was unearned, not a reward.

options.text: Because his sins were really quite minor.
options.note: He calls himself the worst of sinners; mercy met great sin.

options.text: Because he promised to make up for his past.
options.note: Mercy came before and apart from any repayment on Paul's part.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe God in his doxology?

options.text: The King eternal, immortal, and invisible, the only God.
options.note: Verse 17 gives this praise to God.

options.text: A distant force with no interest in individuals.
options.note: The very context shows God's personal mercy to Paul.

options.text: One god among many equals.
options.note: Paul calls Him the only God, not one among many.

options.text: A God who cannot forgive great sinners.
options.note: The whole passage proves the opposite.

quiz.q: What does Paul charge Timothy to do?

options.text: To fight the good fight, holding on to faith and a good conscience.
options.note: Verses 18 and 19 give the charge and its safeguard.

options.text: To avoid all conflict and keep everyone comfortable.
options.note: Ministry is pictured as warfare, not the avoidance of every conflict.

options.text: To trust his gifting and ignore his conscience.
options.note: Rejecting a good conscience is exactly what shipwrecks faith.

options.text: To rely on his own strength alone.
options.note: It is Christ who strengthens; the fight is fought in His grace.

quiz.q: What happened to those who rejected faith and a good conscience?

options.text: They shipwrecked their faith, like Hymenaeus and Alexander.
options.note: Verses 19 and 20 give the warning and name the men.

options.text: They simply grew tired and took a rest.
options.note: This softens a serious warning; they made shipwreck of faith.

options.text: They were rewarded for their independent thinking.
options.note: Rejecting conscience led to ruin, not reward.

options.text: Nothing at all changed for them.
options.note: The consequence was real: shipwrecked faith and church discipline.

questions: Paul never got over the mercy shown to him. Has grace become ordinary to you, or does it still amaze you that Christ came to save sinners like you?
Paul calls himself the worst of sinners, yet full of hope. How does holding both together, honest about sin and confident in mercy, change the way you come to God?
Christ made Paul a pattern to show that no one is beyond reach. Who have you written off as too far gone for the gospel, and how does this passage challenge that?
Timothy is charged to fight the good fight. What does faithful spiritual warfare look like in your life right now, and are you fighting or drifting?
Some shipwrecked their faith by rejecting a good conscience. Where are you tempted to silence your conscience, and what might it cost you if you do?

translationNotes.note: Trustworthy saying renders a fixed formula, pistos ho logos, marking a reliable gospel summary; it recurs in 3:1, 4:9, 2 Timothy 2:11, and Titus 3:8.
The worst of sinners translates a word meaning foremost or chief; Paul uses the present tense, keeping the confession current, not merely past.
Fight the good fight renders military language, literally to wage the good warfare, picturing ministry and the Christian life as a real battle.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Timothy 1:12-20 this session. No em or en dashes occur in this passage.
